name: Foz das Aceas Route
name_original: Río Sarria y Foz das Aceas – circular desde Sarria
description: Hike the moderate 7.0-mile Rio Sarria y Foz das Aceas circular route, exploring lush forests, river views, and historic watermills near Sarr
region_intro: Hiking around Sarria, located in the Galician province of Lugo, Spain, offers trails through verdant, hilly landscapes. The region is characterized by lush forests of chestnut, oak, alder, and maple trees, alongside green meadows and rolling hills. The Sarria River flows through the area, providing riverside paths and featuring gorges like the Foz das Aceas. This terrain provides a variety of experiences for different fitness levels. Best hiking trails around Sarria The most popular hiking route is Rio Sarria y Foz das Aceas - circular from Sarria, a 7.0 miles (11.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 3 minutes to complete. This moderate route follows the Sarria River through the Foz das Aceas gorge, passing dams and mills. Another top favourite among local hikers is Church in Sarria – Sculpture loop from O Souto, a difficult 15.7 miles (25.2 km) path. This route traverses the Galician countryside, passing historic churches and offering views of the rural landscape. Local hikers also love the Stone Bridge Over the Stream – Puente de Áspera loop from Sarria, a 15.5 miles (24.9 km) trail leading through rolling hills and local scenery, often completed in about 6 hours 49 minutes. Hiking around Sarria is defined by lush forests, rolling hills, and riverside paths. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to more challenging long-distance routes. The routes in Sarria are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 100 reviews. More than 1000 hikers have used komoot to explore Sarria's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking around Sarria?
A: Sarria is nestled in the verdant, hilly landscapes of Galicia. You'll hike through lush forests of chestnut, oak, alder, and maple trees, alongside green meadows and rolling hills. The Sarria River also offers beautiful riverside paths, including the scenic Foz das Aceas gorge.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available in Sarria?
A: Yes, Sarria offers several excellent circular routes. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995497537/rio-sarria-y-foz-das-aceas-circular-from-sarria" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rio Sarria y Foz das Aceas - circular from Sarria&lt;/a&gt;, which follows the river through a gorge, passing old dams and mills. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40012109" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Stone Bridge Over the Stream – Puente de Áspera loop from Sarria&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through rolling hills and local scenery.

Q: What is the Camino de Santiago, and how does it relate to Sarria?
A: Sarria is famously the most popular starting point for pilgrims undertaking the final 100 kilometers of the Camino Francés to Santiago de Compostela. This section of the Camino is known for its spiritual atmosphere, passing through charming medieval villages, historic churches, and ancient granaries, offering a varied path through the Galician landscape.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or a relaxed walk?
A: Yes, there are several easy options. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31451056" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de Áspera – View of the trail loop from Sarria&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 6.3 km route. The Ruta da Foz das Aceas, which follows the Sarria River, is also an accessible and pleasant walk, allowing you to enjoy the water, birds, and local flora.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I discover along the trails?
A: Along the trails, especially near the Sarria River, you can find features like five dams, four mills, and a waterfall within the Foz das Aceas gorge. You might also encounter significant natural monuments such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/424987" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ancient Chestnut Tree in Ramil&lt;/a&gt; or an &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5911863" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ancient tree on the Camino Francés, just after Sarria&lt;/a&gt;. Historic churches and medieval villages are also common sights.

Q: Is Sarria a good destination for family-friendly hikes?
A: Yes, Sarria offers trails suitable for families. The Ruta da Foz das Aceas, for example, is an accessible and pleasant walk along the Sarria River, perfect for enjoying nature with children. Many of the easier, shorter routes provide a good experience for all ages.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Sarria?
A: The best time to hike in Sarria is generally during the spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October) when the weather is mild and the landscapes are particularly vibrant. Summer can be pleasant, but it's also the busiest season, especially on the Camino de Santiago. Winters are cooler and wetter, but still offer opportunities for hiking if you are prepared for the conditions.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Sarria?
A: The hiking routes in Sarria are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 100 reviews. Hikers often praise the lush forests, rolling hills, and well-maintained riverside paths, as well as the variety of options for different ability levels.

Q: Are there any challenging or difficult routes for experienced hikers?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers looking for a challenge, Sarria offers more difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38156323" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church in Sarria – Sculpture loop from O Souto&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 25.2 km path that traverses the Galician countryside, offering extensive views and a more demanding experience.

Q: Can I find places to eat or drink near the hiking routes?
A: Sarria, being a significant stop on the Camino de Santiago, has numerous amenities. You'll find cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options within the town and in the smaller villages along the Camino and other local routes, especially in the more populated areas.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on the trails?
A: The lush Galician countryside around Sarria is home to various wildlife. Along the river paths and in the forests, you might spot a variety of bird species. The natural environment supports typical woodland creatures, though larger animals are generally shy and less frequently seen.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural attractions to visit in Sarria?
A: Beyond the trails, Sarria's historic center is worth exploring. It features emblazoned houses, monuments, and former pilgrims' hospitals, showcasing its rich medieval history. You can also visit the viewpoint, Miradoiro do Cárcere, for scenic views of the town and surrounding area.
tips: Bridge that crosses the River Sarria, a nice walk.

Sarria is an important town in Lugo with just over 13,000 inhabitants, located in the region of the same name and one of the most important nerve points of the Camino de Santiago (it connects the French Camino de Santiago with the Camino del Norte). Here tens of thousands of walkers begin their pilgrimage every year for the claim of being the town that marks the minimum distance to be able to achieve the Compostela (it is located 114 kilometers from Santiago).

faqs.question: How difficult is the Rio Sarria y Foz das Aceas circular trail?
faqs.answer: This trail is generally considered moderate, though some sources classify it as easy to low difficulty. While largely a comfortable walk, there are sections with wooden walkways, bridges, and stairs that require navigation. The elevation changes are minimal, making it accessible for most hikers.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this hike?
faqs.answer: The path primarily follows the banks of the Sarria River through a narrow gorge. You'll walk on earth trails, which transition into a shaded path within a dense, native forest. Expect to encounter constructed elements like wooden walkways, handrails, stairs, and bridges to help navigate the route.

faqs.question: What natural features and landmarks will I see along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience a lush riparian forest with continuous views of the Sarria River. The route is known for its historic watermills, locally called 'aceas' or 'muíños', such as Acea de Arriba and Muíño do Conde. You'll also pass by rock walls and, depending on the season, might see a waterfall (fervenza da Cenza). Don't miss the Aceñas Bridge and the nearby La Magdalena Monastery, Sarria.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the circular route?
faqs.answer: The circular route is approximately 6 to 7.9 kilometers long and usually takes around 2 to 2.5 hours to complete at a comfortable pace.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is easily accessible from the center of Sarria, suggesting ample parking options within the town. Specific parking areas are not detailed, but starting from the town center provides convenient access.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for families?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally considered suitable for all ages and fitness levels, making it a great option for families. The minimal elevation gain and varied terrain with constructed elements add to the adventure.

faqs.question: Is the Rio Sarria y Foz das Aceas trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The trail is a popular spot for locals, and while not explicitly stated, many natural trails in Spain are dog-friendly, especially if kept on a leash. It's always best to ensure your dog is well-behaved and to clean up after them.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The lush riparian forest provides ample shade, which is particularly welcome during warmer months. This suggests that spring, summer, and early autumn would all be pleasant times to visit, offering different seasonal beauty.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee for this hike?
faqs.answer: No, there is no mention of any permits or entrance fees required to hike this trail. It appears to be freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: Are there any historical or cultural points of interest on the route?
faqs.answer: Absolutely! The trail is named after the historic watermills ('aceas') that dot the riverbanks, offering a glimpse into the region's past. You'll also find informative panels about local flora and fauna, and the path even passes by a cave known as 'la cueva de Gaspar,' a local legend.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers sections of the O Camiño da Acea de Arriba and the Paseo Río Sarria. It also briefly touches parts of the famous Camino Francés - 07 Cacabelos a Palas de Rei.
